Modern Functions and Logic Flow

Transitioning from traditional JavaScript habits to a modernist TypeScript toolkit.

Agenda

  • Function Syntaxes & Hoisting
  • Arrow Functions & Returns
  • Function Signatures
  • Truthy and Falsy Logic
  • Template Literals
  • Declarative Array Methods
  • Defensive Programming

Function Syntaxes

  • Function Declarations
  • Function Expressions
  • Arrow Functions
// Declaration
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}
There are three distinct forms of functions in TypeScript. We will focus on why arrow functions are the modern standard.

Hoisting and Scope

  • Declarations move to top
  • Expressions stay in place
  • Arrow functions are not hoisted
  • Offers granular scope control
Traditional declarations are moved to the top of memory by the compiler, but expressions and arrow functions are not.

The Modern Standard

  • Prefer arrow functions
  • Lexical 'this' binding
  • Reduced boilerplate code
  • Standard for anonymous functions
const add = (a: number, b: number): number => a + b;
Arrow functions are the modern standard due to their concise syntax and how they handle the 'this' keyword.

Arrow Function Anatomy

  • Parameters on the left
  • Fat arrow operator
  • Body on the right
  • Explicit vs Implicit returns
The anatomy of an arrow function allows for very concise code, especially when dealing with single expressions.

Implicit Returns

  • Omit curly braces
  • Omit 'return' keyword
  • Single-expression functions only
  • Cleaner, more readable code
// Single-line implicit return
const toUpper = (str: string) => str.toUpperCase();
For single-expression functions, the result is returned automatically without the return keyword.

Function Signatures

  • Act as strict contracts
  • Define parameter types
  • Define return types
  • Self-documenting code
Signatures eliminate logic errors by ensuring the function receives and returns exactly what is expected.

Void and Boolean Returns

  • Void: no data returned
  • Boolean: true/false logic
  • Eliminates logic errors
const isEven = (num: number): boolean => num % 2 === 0;

const logMsg = (msg: string): void => console.log(msg);
Use void for functions that perform actions (like logging) without returning data to the caller.

Truthy and Falsy Logic

  • Non-booleans evaluated in conditions
  • TypeScript follows JS rules
  • Everything else is truthy
Unlike languages with strict boolean types, TypeScript evaluates non-boolean data in conditional contexts.

The Six Falsy Values

  • false
  • 0
  • '' (empty string)
  • null, undefined, NaN
let username = ""; 
if (username) {
  // This will not run
}
Memorize these six values. Note that empty arrays and objects are actually truthy!

Logic Gotchas

  • [] and {} are truthy
  • ' ' (space) is truthy
  • Empty string '' is falsy
  • Check array length instead
A common mistake is assuming an empty array is falsy. It is truthy because the object exists.

Template Literals

  • Use backticks (`)
  • Avoid clunky concatenation
  • Easy variable interpolation
  • Better spacing control
Template strings prevent the 'crazy' complexity and spacing errors common with the plus-sign concatenation method.

Interpolation Syntax

  • Use ${variable} syntax
  • Multi-line string support
  • Cleaner code appearance
// Modern template string
console.log(`User: ${name} has a score of: ${score}`);
This is much more readable than using quotes and plus signs to build strings.

Declarative Array Methods

  • Avoid manual 'for' loops
  • Use .find() for searching
  • Use .findIndex() for positions
  • More readable and concise
Moving away from imperative loops to declarative methods is a hallmark of professional-grade craftsmanship.

Searching with .find()

  • Returns first matching element
  • Uses callback functions
  • Cleaner than loop breaks
const foundUser = users.find(u => u.id === targetId);
The .find method is the modern way to locate data within an array without writing boilerplate loop logic.

Strict Null Checks

  • Defensive programming practice
  • Prevents runtime crashes
  • Crucial for DOM interaction
  • Check before accessing properties
Always perform null checks when interacting with the DOM to ensure your application doesn't crash on null variables.

Safe DOM Access

  • QuerySelector can return null
  • Verify element existence first
  • TypeScript enforces safety
const btn = document.querySelector('#btn');
if (btn !== null) {
  btn.textContent = "Click Me";
}
By checking if the button is not null, TypeScript knows it is safe to access its properties inside the block.

Summary

  • Prefer arrow functions always
  • Memorize the six falsy values
  • Use template strings for interpolation
  • Use .find() over manual loops
  • Always perform strict null checks
Adopting these patterns moves you from basic functionality to professional TypeScript development.
